text: Releases are split between two tools on purpose: release-please owns the tag, the GitHub release object, and the release notes/CHANGELOG; goreleaser only builds and uploads artifacts. That's why .goreleaser.yaml sets release.mode: append and changelog.disable: true, and why the workflow passes GORELEASER_CURRENT_TAG explicitly (the goreleaser job runs on main, not on the tag ref). Rejected alternative: letting goreleaser own the release too. We kept release-please because it already manages the version bump PR, the CHANGELOG, and the release body from conventional commits — goreleaser's changelog is weaker and would duplicate that work.

Known flaw in the release job: if goreleaser fails partway through uploading artifacts, a rerun will hit 422 conflicts on the assets that already uploaded and abort. Fix when it bites: set release.replace_existing_artifacts: true in .goreleaser.yaml. Not set today because nobody has hit it yet and the old hand-rolled matrix was just as fragile.

Decided against adding an MCP server (for now). The CLI already serves the agents that matter for repo-level coding work (Claude Code, opencode, Aider, Cursor) since they all have shell access. The agents that only speak MCP (Claude desktop, etc.) are not the primary audience. Costs of building it now: a new dependency on an MCP SDK, a second interface to maintain alongside the CLI for every command added, and coupling to a protocol that is still in flux. The internal/commands package is already factored as functions taking a Store and an io.Writer, so if MCP later becomes unavoidable (e.g. serious coding agents drop shell access in favor of MCP-only), the architecture supports adding it quickly. Reconsider when there is concrete demand from a target agent platform.

Placed the word-wrap helper in internal/textwrap (its own package) rather than as a private function inside internal/commands. The blocking ticket gno-ebehg will use it from show.go, but future commands (e.g. a pager or a formatted export) may also need it. A dedicated package keeps the dependency direction clean: commands can import textwrap, but textwrap imports nothing from commands.

Considered replacing SQLite FTS5 with Bleve for full-text search. Rejected. The main argument for Bleve would be dropping cgo, but we already use modernc.org/sqlite (pure-Go), so static binaries and cross-compilation are not at risk. FTS5 gives us BM25 ranking, porter stemming, unicode61 tokenization, prefix indexes (2/3/4), and snippet() with custom delimiters in ~260 lines and a single-file index that's trivially disposable/rebuildable from the JSONL source of truth. Bleve would add ~30+ transitive deps, a larger binary, a directory-shaped index, and require re-implementing the snippet sentinel trick. The features Bleve would actually add over FTS5 are fuzzy/edit-distance queries and richer field boosting; neither is needed for a per-repo why KB, and typo tolerance is especially low-value because the primary writers are LLMs, which rarely typo. Revisit only if real demand for typo-tolerant search emerges; even then, FTS5 trigram tokenizer or a Levenshtein pass over candidates is likely cheaper than swapping engines.
